Parallel Computing Practical

library(MASS)
library(doParallel)
library(foreach)

Question 1

q1 <- foreach(i = 1:100, .combine = rbind) %do% {
  samp <- rexp(100, 1)
  x_bar <- mean(samp)
  var_x <- var(samp)
  output <- c(mean = x_bar, variance = var_x)
}

q1
                mean  variance
result.1   0.9832338 0.9074039
result.2   1.2267711 1.3522258
result.3   1.0661048 1.0848987
result.4   0.9639064 0.9771623
result.5   1.0754228 1.0854740
result.6   1.0687394 1.0003356
result.7   1.0599083 1.2767373
result.8   1.1554783 1.1637671
result.9   1.0201810 1.3165997
result.10  0.9093400 1.3018986
result.11  1.0017322 1.0921493
result.12  1.0586227 0.8262568
result.13  0.9713603 1.1518415
result.14  0.9603569 0.8782193
result.15  1.1329328 1.2038805

result.59  1.0042232 0.8710265
result.60  1.0031647 1.8844885
result.61  1.0207520 1.0008845

result.75  1.0646566 1.2746504

result.98  1.1649541 1.3144163
result.99  1.0653658 0.9196335
result.100 1.0700648 1.0933598

Question 2

Parallel processing

Single bootstrap sample

library(doParallel)

gal <- galaxies
n <- length(gal)

c1 <- makeCluster(2)
registerDoParallel(c1)

system.time( {
  result_parallel <- foreach(i = 1:1000, .combine = c) %dopar% {
  samp <- sample(gal, n, replace = TRUE)
  med <- median(samp)
  }
})
   user  system elapsed 
   0.19    0.03    0.32 
stopCluster(c1)

1000 bootstrap samples

c2 <- makeCluster(2)
registerDoParallel(c2)

system.time( {
  results_large_p <- foreach(i = 1:100, .combine = c) %dopar% {
  vec <- numeric(1000)
  
  for (j in 1:1000) {
    s <- sample(gal, n, replace = TRUE)
    vec[j] <- median(s)
  }
  
  }
})
   user  system elapsed 
   0.08    0.00    2.47 
stopCluster(c2)

Serial processing

Single bootstrap sample

system.time( {
  result_serial <- foreach(i = 1:1000, .combine = c) %do% {
    boot_samp <- sample(gal, n, replace = TRUE)
    med_samp <- median(boot_samp)
  }
})
   user  system elapsed 
   0.22    0.02    0.23

1000 bootstrap samples

system.time( {
  results_large_s <- foreach(i = 1:100, .combine = c) %do% {
  vec <- numeric(1000)
  
  for (j in 1:1000) {
    s <- sample(gal, n, replace = TRUE)
    vec[j] <- median(s)
  }
  
  }
})
   user  system elapsed 
   4.51    0.11    4.61

From the results above, we see that the total run time for parallel processing is higher than for serial processing. However, with larger chunks we can see that using a parallel approach significantly decreases the processing time, thereby improving efficiency compared to serial processing.

Question 3

c3 <- makeCluster(4)
registerDoParallel(c3)

boot <- foreach(i = 1:10, .combine = rbind) %dopar% {

  exp_samp <- rexp(50, 1)
  boot_means <- numeric(100)
    
  for (j in 1:100) {
    b_sample <- sample(exp_samp, 50, replace = TRUE)
    boot_means[j] <- mean(b_sample) 
  }
  
  sorted <- sort(boot_means)
  lower <- sorted[ceiling(0.025*100)]
  upper <- sorted[ceiling(0.975*100)]
    
  boot_int <- quantile(boot_means, c(0.025, 0.975))
  
as.numeric(lower <= 1 && upper >= 1)

}

(coverage <- mean(boot))
[1] 0.9
stopCluster(c3)

Question 4

library(iterators)

set.seed(1234)

iter1 <- irnorm(5)
iter2 <- irnorm(5)
iter3 <- irnorm(5)

c4 <- makeCluster(3)
registerDoParallel(c4)

result <- foreach(i = iter1, j = iter2, k = iter3, .combine = rbind) %dopar% { 
  c("vector 1" = i, "vector 2" = j, "vector 3" = k) 
}

 max(result[ ,1])
 max(result[ ,2])
 max(result[ ,3])
stopCluster(c4)

Question 5

library(parallel)
library(foreach)
library(doParallel)

set.seed(1234)
v1 <- rnorm(5)
v2 <- rnorm(5)
v3 <- rnorm(5)

vectors <- list(v1, v2, v3)

c5 <- makeCluster(3)
registerDoParallel(c5)

Time for parLapply

system.time({
  res_par <- parLapply(c5, vectors, max)
})
   user  system elapsed 
      0       0       0

Time for replicate

system.time({
  result_rep <- replicate(3, max(rnorm(5)))
})
   user  system elapsed 
      0       0       0

Time for foreach

system.time({
  res_foreach <- foreach(v = vectors) %dopar% {
    max(v)
  }
})
   user  system elapsed 
   0.00    0.00    0.03 
stopCluster(c5)

From the above results, we see that the replicate function is the most efficient, with the smallest elapsed time. It is a sequential function, which usually works better for smaller data. The least efficient is parLapply, which is slightly slower than the foreach function.
